title: Carved hazel cooking skewers
subtitle: Sharpened hazel sticks for open-fire cooking from a winter coppice cut
excerpt: Hazel skewers cut fresh from a winter coppice are the oldest and simplest camp and firepit cooking tool: straight, smooth-barked, long enough to hold food clear of the fire, and free. They are cut, pointed, and ready to use in ten minutes. Any leftover skewers that have dried can be soaked in water before use to slow charring. This beginner project covers selecting the right poles, stripping the bark, and cutting the point.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the use of hazel sticks in outdoor and open-fire cooking. Cassell's Household Guide (1869) for the camp-cooking tradition of green-wood skewers.

content.text: A
green

hazel skewer cut from the current year's coppice growth is the simplest possible wood carving project and one of the most directly useful: fresh hazel, with its moisture still in the wood, does not char readily over a fire. The smooth, straight shoots of second-year hazel growth are the ideal diameter for a cooking skewer. The project takes ten minutes and requires only a sloyd knife.
What you need: wood

Straight hazel poles, 60 to 80 cm long, 8 to 12 mm in diameter, from second-year coppice growth. The poles should be straight along at least 50 cm of their length. Freshly cut material is preferred; the skewers can be made at the coppice on the day of cutting and used immediately. Birch and willow are equally good alternatives; avoid elder (hollow pith), ash (too stiff to point cleanly when green), and yew (poisonous).
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife, kept sharp with a leather strop. No other tools are needed.
Safety
Point the skewer away from the body using a
push cut
; never point toward the holding hand or toward bystanders. Keep fingers curled around the stick, not extended near the blade path.
The work
Step 1: Remove side branches

Trim all side branches flush with the main pole with the sloyd knife. Hold the pole with the base toward the free hand and trim branch stubs with a slicing cut angled away from the body. A flush trim leaves a smooth surface; a stub left proud will catch on food and is uncomfortable to handle.
Step 2: Strip the bark from the tip end

Strip the bark from the final 20 cm of the skewer. Hold the pole horizontally with the tip end pointing away. Lay the blade flat against the pole surface and push it forward along the length; the blade edge lifts the bark cleanly without cutting into the wood. Stripping the tip end removes the outer bark that would char first in fire and exposes the pale green wood underneath.
Step 3: Point the tip

With the pole held in the non-knife hand, take push cuts away from the body to whittle the tip to a point. Four angled cuts, one from each quarter of the pole diameter, produce a four-faceted point. The point should be 2 to 3 cm long; a longer point is fragile and likely to split under food load. The tip does not need to be needle-sharp; a 3 mm flat tip is sufficient to pierce most foods.
Step 4: Cut the handle end flat

Trim the handle end square with a single cut. The flat handle end is easier to hold than a rough broken end and allows the skewer to rest on the edge of a grill or fire pit with the food suspended above the embers.
Variations

A forked skewer for sausages: instead of a single point, split the final 5 cm of the stick with the knife to produce a two-pronged fork. The split is easier to make across the grain at the tip end of a green hazel pole.

A cooking tripod for a pot: three thick poles (25 to 30 mm diameter, 120 cm long) pointed at the lower ends and lashed together near the top can support a hanging pot over a fire. The same hazel pole selection criteria apply.
Care

Use fresh. If making skewers a day ahead of use, soak them in water for 30 minutes before using over fire; this restores the moisture content that slows charring. Do not store skewers in contact with food; they are single-use or at most a few-use items.

items.fix: Whittle the point to no more than 3 cm length and keep the tip diameter at 3 to 4 mm rather than tapered to a needle. Thicker poles (10 to 12 mm diameter) are stronger at the tip than thin ones.
items.cause: The point was cut too long and thin, or the pole diameter was too small.
items.symptom: The tip splits along the grain when piercing hard food

items.fix: Soak dried skewers in water for 30 minutes before use. Hold food above the embers rather than in direct flame.
items.cause: The pole was dry rather than freshly cut, or the tip was held too close to the embers rather than above them.
items.symptom: The skewer chars at the tip during cooking
